posts.author: quakes99

posts.text: To be clear on the table: the total is right — the robbery row is a YTD copy-paste error. But the typo isn’t the real story. The comparison method is. These releases stack 2026’s preliminary counts against 2025 figures quietly revised upward since first reported. Watch what that does to felony assault — the biggest violent crime category. Last July, NYPD reported 7,843 for Q2. This July, that same quarter is listed as 8,136. Against the inflated baseline, 2026’s 8,044 reads as ‘decreased 1.1%.’ Against what was actually reported last year, it’s UP 2.6%. Every category gets this treatment. Apples to apples, violent crime is roughly flat, not falling. No public company could get away with this: comparing this quarter’s soft numbers to last year’s restated ones and calling the difference ‘performance.’ Investors would call it misleading and the SEC would call it securities fraud. Cc @NYPDPC Jessica Tisch
posts.author: BarbarianCap
